data.source: Online experiments with Amazon Mechanical Turk (MTurk) participants; real and hypothetical payoffs used across studies
data.wave_or_subset: Four experiments (Experiments 1–4) with varying frames, outcomes, and endowment status manipulations
data.sample_size: Experiment 1: N=119; Experiment 2: N=418; Experiment 3: N=533; Experiment 4: N=605
data.notes: Participants completed instructional manipulation checks and comprehension questions; inclusion limited to those who passed checks for main analyses. WTP distributions deviated from normality, so both parametric (t-tests, ANOVA) and nonparametric tests (Mann–Whitney, Kruskal–Wallis) were used. Experiment 1 used incentive-compatible purchasing (five randomly selected participants received a $20 bonus and an opportunity to purchase at a reservation price). Consistent exclusion criteria were adopted across experiments.

method.description: Across four MTurk-based experiments, participants evaluated two-outcome prospects framed with labels varying in association with risk (e.g., lottery, raffle, coin flip, gamble vs. gift certificate, voucher). The studies elicited willingness to pay (WTP) and willingness to accept (WTA) for risky and certain prospects while manipulating the low and high outcome levels and endowment status. Analyses tested how framing, outcome levels, and buyer/seller status influence valuations.
steps: Recruit MTurk participants and obtain consent.

Randomly assign participants to conditions varying by frame (e.g., lottery, raffle, coin flip, gamble, gift certificate, voucher), outcome levels (e.g., low=$25 or $50; high=$100 or $200), objective uncertainty (risky vs. certain), and endowment status (buyer vs. seller).
Administer instructional manipulation checks and comprehension questions; include only participants who pass for primary analyses.
Elicit WTP (buyers) or WTA (sellers) in dollars for the described prospect.
Experiment 1: Implement incentive-compatible mechanism for a subset (five randomly selected participants with a $20 bonus and purchase opportunity at a hidden reservation price).
Analyze data using parametric (t-tests, ANOVA) and nonparametric tests (Mann–Whitney, Kruskal–Wallis); report effect sizes and significance.
Experiment 4: Assess processing disfluency to rule out comprehension differences for unusual certain frames.
method.models: t-tests; ANOVA (including interaction terms); chi-square tests for comprehension; nonparametric Mann–Whitney and Kruskal–Wallis tests; planned contrasts; effect sizes reported as partial eta-squared
method.outcome_variable: Willingness to Pay (WTP) and Willingness to Accept (WTA), measured in US dollars
method.independent_variables: Frame label (gift certificate, voucher, coin flip, raffle, lottery ticket, gamble); outcome levels (low=$25 or $50; high=$100 or $200); endowment status (buyer vs. seller); objective uncertainty (risky prospect vs. certain low outcome)

results.summary: Across four experiments, framing affected WTP but not WTA. Frames associated with risk (lottery, raffle, coin flip, gamble) substantially reduced WTP compared with frames not associated with risk (gift certificate, voucher), independent of objective uncertainty. Buyers’ WTP was sensitive to the lowest possible outcome and to risk-associated frames but not to the highest outcome. Sellers’ WTA was sensitive to both lowest and highest outcomes but not to framing. When risky and certain prospects were mismatched in frames (risky prospect labeled with high-risk frame; low outcome labeled with low-risk frame), many participants exhibited the uncertainty effect, valuing the risky prospect less than its certain worst outcome.

metadata.original_paper_title: Framing Influences Willingness to Pay but Not Willingness to Accept
